history.text: After an abortive attempt in 1688–89, in 1717, the Hudson's Bay Company built the first permanent settlement, Churchill River Post, a log fort a few kilometres upstream from the mouth of the Churchill River.
As part of the Anglo-French dispute for North America, in 1731–1741, the original fort was replaced with Prince of Wales Fort, a large stone fort on the western peninsula at the mouth of the.
Initially, Port Nelson was selected for this purpose in 1912.
In 1942, the United States Army Air Forces established a base called Fort Churchill, 8 km (5.0 mi) east of the town.
Around 1949, Churchill became part of the Canadian SUPRAD (signals intelligence) network and remained in that role until it closed its doors in 1968.

history.label: Today
history.text: Since before the time of European contact, the region around Churchill has been predominantly inhabited by the Chipewyan and Cree peoples.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: The discoveries were all taken to Denmark; some are on display at the National Museum in Copenhagen.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: The fort, Prince of Wales Fort, was rebuilt at the mouth of the river.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: The fort was primarily built to capitalize on the North American fur trade, out of the reach of York Factory.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: A defensive battery, Cape Merry Battery, was built on the opposite side of the fort to provide protection.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: After several years of effort and millions of dollars, this project was abandoned, and Churchill was chosen as the alternative after World War One.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Once this transportation rail link from farms to the Churchill port was completed, commercial shipping took many more years to pick up.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Following the demolition of the base it was repurposed into the town's airport.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Naval Radio Station Churchill, call sign CFL, was activated as an ionospheric study station by the Royal Canadian Navy in support of the U-boat high-frequency direction finding (HFDF) net and became operational on 1.
